# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 80

This is turn 80. Turns 1 through 79 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 80.

## Turn 80 — Thompson critical subgroups collapse the Frattini-radical tower

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit. In the proof, the exponent is the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); \(40491355905\) is its prescribed verifier residue. 

Turn 79 correctly disproved the assertion
\[
K/\Phi(K)\cong V.
\]
The correct top quotient can instead be an indecomposable module \(M\) with simple head \(V\) and scalar radical. What Turn 79 left open was whether the deeper subgroup \(\Phi(K)\) could require an arbitrarily long Frattini-radical analysis. 

It does not. The missing tool is the Thompson critical-subgroup theorem.

### 1. The minimal active subgroup is itself 2-critical

Retain the Turn-76/79 notation. Thus
\[
S\triangleleft P\triangleleft G,\qquad P/S=V
\]
is the first nontrivial tame chief factor above a scalar run, and
\[
K\triangleleft G,\qquad KS=P
\]
is minimal with this property.

Let \(N\) be the odd normal subgroup of the fixed tame quotient whose action on \(V\) is nontrivial, and choose an odd Hall lift \(\widetilde N\) in \(G\).

Use the standard \(p=2\) critical-subgroup theorem in the following form:

> Every finite \(2\)-group \(K\) has a characteristic subgroup \(C\) such that
> \[
> C/Z(C)\ \text{is elementary abelian},\qquad C^4=1,
> \]
> and the kernel of
> \[
> \operatorname{Aut}(K)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(C)
> \]
> is a \(2\)-group.

The group \(\widetilde N\) centralizes \(S\). Indeed, it acts trivially on every factor of a \(G\)-chief series for \(S\), and the automorphisms acting trivially on such a \(2\)-series form a \(2\)-group; an odd subgroup of that kernel is trivial.

On the other hand, \(\widetilde N\) acts nontrivially on
\[
K/(K\cap S)\cong V.
\]
It therefore acts nontrivially on \(K\). By the critical-subgroup theorem it cannot centralize \(C\). Hence \(C\nleq S\).

Because \(C\) is characteristic in \(K\), it is \(G\)-normal. Its image in the simple module \(P/S=V\) is nonzero, so
\[
CS=P.
\]
Minimality of \(K\) now forces
\[
C=K.
\]

Consequently
\[
K/Z(K)\ \text{is elementary abelian},\qquad K^4=1.
\]
Writing
\[
R=\Phi(K)=K^2[K,K],
\]
we obtain
\[
R\le Z(K),\qquad R^2=1.
\tag{80.1}
\]
Thus \(R\) is elementary abelian and \(K\) has class at most two and exponent at most four.

So Turn 79 invalidated the claim that the *top module is simple*, but not the structural class-two collapse. Its \(A_4\)-augmentation witness has \(R=1\), and is fully compatible with (80.1).

### 2. The correct top module is enough

Put
\[
M=K/R,\qquad T=(K\cap S)/R.
\]
Turn 79 proved
\[
T=\operatorname{rad}(M),\qquad \operatorname{head}(M)\cong V,
\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{G/K}(M,\mathbf F_2)=0.
\tag{80.2}
\]

Turn 78 already shows that the elementary layer
\[
1\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow G/R\longrightarrow G/K\longrightarrow1
\]
is unobstructed and has exactly
\[
2^{2\dim M}
\]
lifts over every generated lower tuple. Its deformation-complex argument applies to arbitrary elementary modules, including this nonsemisimple \(M\). 

It remains to justify the scalar Fourier pushouts through \(R\).

### 3. Simple-head determinant lemma

Let
\[
0\ne\lambda\in (R^\vee)^{G/R}.
\]
The pushout
\[
K_\lambda=K/\ker\lambda
\]
is a central extension
\[
1\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2
 \longrightarrow K_\lambda
 \longrightarrow M
 \longrightarrow1.
\]
Since \(\ker\lambda\le\Phi(K)\),
\[
\Phi(K_\lambda)=R/\ker\lambda\ne1,
\]
so this extension is nonsplit.

Let
\[
q_\lambda:M\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2
\]
be its quadratic map and \(b_\lambda\) its polar form.

The map
\[
b_\lambda^\flat:M\longrightarrow M^\vee
\]
kills \(T\). Indeed, every composition factor of \(T\) is trivial, while
\[
\operatorname{soc}(M^\vee)\cong
\operatorname{head}(M)^\vee\cong V^\vee
\]
is nontrivial simple. Hence every \(G/K\)-map \(T\to M^\vee\) is zero.

The important new point is
\[
q_\lambda|_T=0.
\tag{80.3}
\]

Suppose instead that
\[
\ell=q_\lambda|_T\ne0.
\]
Set \(T_1=\ker\ell\) and
\[
\overline M=M/T_1.
\]
Then
\[
0\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2 t
\longrightarrow\overline M
\longrightarrow V
\longrightarrow0,
\]
where \(t\) spans the polar radical and
\[
\overline q(t)=1.
\]

The lower wild \(2\)-kernel acts trivially on \(t\) and on \(V\). Any such automorphism of \(\overline M\) therefore has the form
\[
x\longmapsto x+f(\bar x)t.
\]
But invariance of \(\overline q\) gives
\[
\overline q(x+f(\bar x)t)
=\overline q(x)+f(\bar x),
\]
so \(f=0\). Thus the lower wild kernel acts trivially on \(\overline M\), and the module factors through the finite tame quotient \(H\).

For every nontrivial simple tame module,
\[
\operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbf F_2[H]}(V,\mathbf F_2)
 \cong H^1(H,V^\vee)=0.
\tag{80.4}
\]
If \(V\) is ramified, this follows from \(V^I=0\) and the odd order of tame inertia. If \(V\) is unramified, it follows from the cyclic formula because Frobenius minus the identity is invertible on \(V^\vee\).

Hence \(\overline M\cong\mathbf F_2\oplus V\), producing a nonzero \(G/K\)-map
\[
M\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2,
\]
contrary to (80.2). This proves (80.3).

Therefore \(q_\lambda\) descends to a quadratic form
\[
\bar q_\lambda:V\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2.
\]
It is nonzero because \(K_\lambda\) is nonsplit. Its polar form cannot vanish, since a zero-polar invariant form on the nontrivial simple module \(V\) would be an invariant linear form. Thus its polar form is nondegenerate.

We have proved the corrected replacement for Turn 76:

> Every nonzero invariant scalar pushout of \(R=\Phi(K)\) is a nonsingular determinant extension of the simple head \(V\), even though \(M=K/R\) itself need not be simple.

### 4. Why the intervening scalar radical causes no new obstruction

Let
\[
B=G/R,\qquad C=G/K.
\]
The central pushout class
\[
\kappa_\lambda\in H^2(B,\mathbf F_2)
\]
restricts to \(q_\lambda\) on \(M\).

First filter through
\[
T\triangleleft B.
\]
Because both \(q_\lambda|_T\) and its polar form on \(T\) vanish, the restriction component is zero.

If the filtration-one edge over \(T\) is nonzero, Turn 78’s deformation-complex duality makes it a nonzero affine functional on the \(T\)-lift torsor. Exactly half of the lifts survive, uniformly on the candidate and local sides.

If that edge is zero, \(\kappa_\lambda\) inflates to \(B/T\). Now
\[
1\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow B/T\longrightarrow C\longrightarrow1.
\]
Since \(\bar q_\lambda\) survives and has nonsingular polar form,
\[
0=d_2(\bar q_\lambda)
   =\bar b_\lambda^\flat{}_*(\eta)
\]
forces the \(V\)-extension class \(\eta\) to vanish. Thus this exposed \(V\)-layer is split.

The remaining central problem is precisely the already-solved split simple determinant problem: filtration-one terms are removed by shearing, and the determinant zero count agrees by the Wall–Arf and local deep-unit theorems.

Hence, for every nonzero \(\lambda\), the scalar pushout obstruction has the same zero count for the candidate and \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\).

### 5. Fourier reconstruction is restored

For the full elementary central kernel \(R\), Turn 78 identifies
\[
D=(R^\vee)^B
\]
with the complete dual of the relation-obstruction space on both sides.

If \(X_\Gamma\) is the fixed-frame lower-map set and
\[
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}
=\#\{\rho\in X_\Gamma:
       \langle\lambda,o_\Gamma(\rho)\rangle=0\},
\]
then
\[
\#\{\rho:o_\Gamma(\rho)=0\}
=
\frac1{|D|}
\sum_{\lambda\in D}
\bigl(2m_{\Gamma,\lambda}-|X_\Gamma|\bigr).
\tag{80.5}
\]

For \(\lambda=0\), equality follows by induction on the smaller wild kernel \(L/R\). For every \(\lambda\ne0\), it follows from the simple-head determinant lemma above. Thus every term of (80.5) agrees.

Once the obstruction vanishes, Turn 78 gives the same lift multiplicity on both sides. Scalar decorations annihilate \(R=\Phi(K)\), and every lift is surjective: if \(C<G\) mapped onto \(G/R\), then
\[
(C\cap K)R=K,
\]
so \(C\cap K=K\) by the Frattini property, forcing \(C=G\).

This restores the Fourier block without assuming
\[
K/\Phi(K)\cong V.
\]

The genuine theorem is instead:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
K/\Phi(K)\ \text{has simple head }V
\text{ and scalar radical},\\
K\ \text{is nevertheless class two of exponent at most four},\\
\Phi(K)\ \text{is central elementary},\\
\text{and every invariant scalar pushout is a determinant pushout of }V.
\end{gathered}}
\]

Thus the Frattini-radical tower proposed at the end of Turn 79 collapses after one step. The wild unipotent shearing enlarges the top elementary module, but it cannot propagate into a deeper nonlinear Frattini tower.

**ESTABLISHED**: Applied the Thompson \(2\)-critical-subgroup theorem to the minimal \(N\)-active subgroup \(K\). Minimality forces the critical subgroup to equal \(K\), proving that \(K\) has class at most two and exponent at most four and that \(R=\Phi(K)\) is central elementary. Proved the simple-head determinant lemma: although \(M=K/R\) may have a scalar radical, every nonzero invariant scalar pushout vanishes on that radical and descends to a nonsingular determinant form on the simple head \(V\). Combined this with Turn 78 deformation duality and Fourier inversion to close the mixed Frattini block uniformly.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun. The argument now needs publication-grade consolidation, particularly a formal statement of the Thompson critical-subgroup input and the simple-head determinant lemma inside the tame-framed theorem; no structural finite-quotient branch is presently open.

**NEXT**: Package Turns 74, 78, 79, and 80 as one fixed-tame-frame induction theorem, sum over tame frames, specialize to \(L=O_2(G)\), and submit the unchanged \(A_2\) residue block to the official verifier.

[BREAKTHROUGH]
